segments.text_preview: You're in your Brooklyn apartment. It's 3 PM. You've been in bed since noon. The weight isn't just in your body—it's in the air...
segments.scene_description: a quiet bedroom in a Brooklyn apartment in afternoon light, rumpled bedcovers, window with soft daylight, empty space suggesting someone has been lying there, no figure or face visible, contemplative and heavy mood

segments.text_preview: Depression doesn't always look like crying. Sometimes it looks like scrolling. Sometimes it looks like canceling plans again...
segments.scene_description: a smartphone screen glowing in a dim room, one hand holding the phone, suggestion of a social feed or notifications, muted cool tones, no face visible, sense of staying in while others are out

segments.text_preview: Your brain might tell you you're broken. That you should be doing more. That everyone else has it figured out...
segments.scene_description: abstract suggestion of inner noise or a critical voice: soft shadows and a single muted light in darkness, like a thought or weight, no literal brain or face, metaphorical and subdued

segments.text_preview: Here's what actually helps: one small move. Not fixing everything. Just one thing that feels slightly less impossible...
segments.scene_description: a single hand reaching toward a simple object—a glass of water on a bedside table or a window latch—soft daylight, one small gesture of movement, hopeful and minimal, no face

segments.text_preview: Maybe it's opening the window. Maybe it's one sip of water. Maybe it's texting one person. Maybe it's standing in the shower...
segments.scene_description: a window cracked open with soft daylight streaming in, or a glass of water on a windowsill with city light behind, small concrete comforts, gentle and specific, no face

segments.text_preview: You're not lazy. You're not failing. You're carrying something heavy in a city that never stops moving. NYC doesn't give you a break...
segments.scene_description: a quiet moment in an urban setting: view through a window of city haze or rooftops, or a still interior with city light at the edge, one hand at rest on a sill or knee, self-compassion not hustle, no face

segments.text_preview: One small move. That's the reset. Not the whole day. Just the next 60 seconds. You've got this. One thing. Then you can rest again.
segments.scene_description: a single small gesture of pause and reset: hand resting gently on a surface, or soft light through a curtain, suggesting the next 60 seconds and permission to rest, hopeful and quiet close, no face
